my_thoughts: The analogy confirmation works: exactly—criminal = made better (like sick body = better for treatment). The student asked clarification question (genuine curiosity), which shows they understand: analogy needs verification (criminal = made better?). They'll likely accept the analogy or ask deeper question next. Hypothesis: analogy confirmations lead to acceptance or deeper questions—testable by tracking whether confirmations lead to acceptance or deeper inquiries.
accumulated_insight: Two-turn pattern: T1: Student asked mechanism question ('How can punishment benefit the one who suffers it?')—seeking clarity via requests clarification. They understand: punishment = suffering (needs explanation of benefit). The structural_analogy mechanism introduces: punishment = cure (like medicine for body), soul = can be diseased, reframes: punishment as healing (not just suffering). T2: Student asked clarification question ('You're saying the criminal is made better by suffering?')—genuine curiosity via asks deeper question. They understand: analogy needs verification (criminal = made better?). The structural_analogy mechanism confirms: exactly—criminal = made better (like sick body = better for treatment), extends: analogy ('Exactly—just as the sick body is better for painful treatment'). Pattern: mechanism question → analogy introduction → clarification request → confirmation → [next: acceptance or deeper question]. They're grasping: punishment = healing (analogy), criminal = made better (confirmation).
visible_response: Exactly—just as the sick body is better for painful treatment.
